threats.title: PermissionValidator Out-of-Bounds Calldata Read Bypasses Session Permission Rules
threats.hypothesis_description: PermissionValidator.validatePermission reads call.data at rule.offset using LibBytes.readBytes32, which does not check if the offset is within the call.data slice bounds. calldataload reads from the raw transaction calldata, not just the slice. An attacker can craft call.data shorter than the required offset, so the PermissionValidator reads adjacent calldata (from other payload fields or signature data) that satisfies the permission rule, while the target contract receiving the actual call.data would see zero-padded bytes at that offset. This discrepancy allows bypassing permission constraints on session keys.
threats.investigation_rationale: LibBytes.readBytes32 uses raw calldataload without bounds checking. The calldata slice for call.data is a bounded view, but calldataload ignores slice boundaries. The attacker controls the full calldata layout and can position satisfying bytes at the OOB read location. This is a concrete, exploitable discrepancy between validation-time and execution-time data views.

proof_of_signal.pattern: call.data.readBytes32(rule.offset) uses calldataload without checking rule.offset < call.data.length; adjacent calldata readable

preconditions: Wallet uses SessionManager with explicit session permissions containing parameter rules
Permission rule has rule.offset that can exceed actual call.data length
Session key holder crafts payload with short call.data and positions matching bytes in adjacent calldata

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: calldataload at arbitrary offset within calldata slice without bounds check
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: readBytes32 does calldataload(add(_pointer, _data.offset)) regardless of _data.length; reads adjacent calldata when _pointer >= _data.length

analysis_questions: Can the attacker reliably position specific bytes at the OOB read offset in the SessionManager calldata?
What permission rule configurations are most exploitable?
Does the ABI encoding layout make precise positioning feasible?
validation_requirements: Construct a PoC where call.data is shorter than rule.offset but adjacent calldata satisfies the rule
Verify the target contract receives only call.data (zero-padded) at the same offset
Demonstrate a concrete permission bypass scenario

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Session key with permission rule checking offset 100 = VALUE. Attacker sends call.data of 36 bytes. At calldata position call.data.offset+100, attacker places VALUE (from another call's data or signature bytes). PermissionValidator sees VALUE and passes. Target contract at offset 100 sees bytes32(0).
prerequisites: Parameter rule offset exceeds minimum call.data length needed for the actual target function

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Session key can bypass all parameter-level permission restrictions, potentially executing unauthorized token transfers or contract calls beyond intended limits
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All wallets using explicit session keys with parameter rules
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Revoke session key or update wallet config

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for OOB offset reads in permission validation with short call.data

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing call.data.length vs rule.offset in transaction trace

threats.title: Unchecked Weight Overflow in recoverBranch Causes DoS on Subdigest Configurations
threats.hypothesis_description: BaseSig.recoverBranch operates in an unchecked block. When FLAG_SUBDIGEST or FLAG_SIGNATURE_ANY_ADDRESS_SUBDIGEST matches, weight is set to type(uint256).max. Any subsequent signer in the SAME branch that adds weight via weight += addrWeight causes uint256 overflow, wrapping weight to a small value (e.g., type(uint256).max + 1 = 0). This causes weight < threshold, rejecting an otherwise valid signature. Any wallet configuration that places a hardcoded subdigest alongside other signers in the same merkle branch is vulnerable to permanent DoS for that subdigest.
threats.investigation_rationale: The unchecked block at line ~151 of BaseSig.sol covers the entire recoverBranch while loop. The weight += addrWeight pattern appears in FLAG_SIGNATURE_HASH, FLAG_ERC1271, FLAG_BRANCH, FLAG_NESTED, FLAG_SIGNATURE_ETH_SIGN, FLAG_SIGNATURE_SAPIENT, and FLAG_SIGNATURE_SAPIENT_COMPACT handlers. All of these execute after FLAG_SUBDIGEST could set weight to max.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unchecked block wraps entire recoverBranch loop; weight = type(uint256).max followed by weight += addrWeight causes overflow

preconditions: Wallet configuration includes a hardcoded subdigest leaf AND at least one other signer leaf in the same merkle branch
The subdigest matches the current operation hash
The other signer(s) provide valid signatures

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: BaseSig.sol lines 151, 261-263, and subsequent weight += lines
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: weight = type(uint256).max in FLAG_SUBDIGEST, then weight += addrWeight in subsequent signer in unchecked block
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: type(uint256).max + N overflows to N-1 in unchecked arithmetic
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No overflow check or cap on weight after setting to max

analysis_questions: Can a realistic wallet configuration place subdigest and other signers in the same branch?
Is there a recommended configuration structure that avoids this?
Does this affect FLAG_SIGNATURE_ANY_ADDRESS_SUBDIGEST equally?
validation_requirements: Create a configuration with subdigest + EOA in same branch
Demonstrate that a valid subdigest match + valid EOA signature results in weight < threshold
Verify the transaction reverts despite valid authorization

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Wallet config has threshold=1, one branch containing: hardcoded subdigest (for emergency operation) and one EOA (weight=1). When the subdigest matches, weight = type(uint256).max. EOA signature adds weight += 1, causing overflow to 0. weight(0) < threshold(1) → signature rejected. Emergency operation permanently blocked.
prerequisites: Wallet configuration with subdigest and other signer leaves sharing a merkle branch

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Denial of service - legitimate operations matching hardcoded subdigests become permanently unexecutable
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Wallets with subdigest configurations containing co-located signers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Wallet owner must update configuration to isolate subdigest in its own branch

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for weight overflow after subdigest match with additional signers in same branch

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Signature recovery would revert with InvalidSignatureWeight

threats.title: ecrecover Returning address(0) Contributes Weight to Signature Tree
threats.hypothesis_description: In BaseSig.recoverBranch, FLAG_SIGNATURE_HASH and FLAG_SIGNATURE_ETH_SIGN do not check if ecrecover returns address(0) (indicating an invalid signature). Weight is unconditionally added, and a leaf for address(0) is included in the merkle root. If a wallet configuration accidentally or intentionally includes address(0) as a signer with non-zero weight, any invalid ECDSA signature would be treated as a valid signature from address(0), granting its weight without requiring any private key.
threats.investigation_rationale: ecrecover returns address(0) for invalid parameters (v not in {27,28}, s=0, etc). The code adds weight and computes leaf without checking addr != address(0). The leaf _leafForAddressAndWeight(address(0), weight) would need to be in the configuration's merkle tree for the imageHash to match.
deployment_assumptions: Wallet configuration must include address(0) as a signer leaf
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence of address(0) signers; risk is in potential misconfiguration

proof_of_signal.pattern: ecrecover result used directly without address(0) check; weight += addrWeight unconditional

preconditions: Wallet configuration includes address(0) as a signer leaf in merkle tree
address(0) has sufficient weight to meet threshold (alone or combined)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: address addr = ecrecover(...); weight += addrWeight; root = ... _leafForAddressAndWeight(addr, addrWeight)
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No check for addr == address(0) after ecrecover
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Missing require(addr != address(0)) or skip-weight-on-zero-address check

analysis_questions: Can the SDK or factory accidentally create configurations with address(0) signers?
Is there any on-chain validation preventing address(0) in signer configs?
What is the realistic likelihood of this misconfiguration?
validation_requirements: Create a wallet config with address(0) as a signer
Submit an invalid ECDSA signature that causes ecrecover to return address(0)
Verify the signature validation passes

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If wallet config includes address(0) with weight >= threshold, any invalid compact signature (r=0, s=0) → ecrecover returns address(0) → weight added → imageHash matches → full wallet control without any private key.
prerequisites: Wallet configuration must include address(0) as a signer

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete wallet takeover if address(0) has sufficient weight

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying address(0) ecrecover result is rejected

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: On-chain: detectable by checking signer config for address(0)

threats.title: Blacklist Allows Duplicate Entries Due to Strict Less-Than Sort Check
threats.hypothesis_description: In SessionSig.recoverConfiguration, the blacklist sort validation uses strict less-than (sig.implicitBlacklist[i] < previousAddress) instead of less-than-or-equal. This allows duplicate consecutive addresses in the blacklist. Additionally, address(0) passes as the first entry since previousAddress starts at address(0). Duplicate entries alter the blacklist's hash, creating a different imageHash than intended, and waste configuration space. More critically, if the blacklist hash changes due to duplicates, the imageHash would not match the wallet's expected configuration, potentially causing signature validation failures.
threats.investigation_rationale: The comparison at the blacklist parsing explicitly uses < instead of <=, which is a common off-by-one pattern in sorted list validation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (sig.implicitBlacklist[i] < previousAddress) revert; uses < instead of <=

preconditions: Session configuration includes a blacklist
Blacklist constructed with duplicate entries

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: strict less-than comparison allows duplicate consecutive addresses
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Duplicates allowed in blacklist; address(0) accepted as first entry

analysis_questions: Can duplicate blacklist entries cause the binary search in _isAddressBlacklisted to miss entries?
Does address(0) in blacklist cause issues with the binary search?
Can duplicates be used to manipulate the blacklist hash?
validation_requirements: Test binary search correctness with duplicate entries
Verify imageHash computation with duplicate vs non-duplicate blacklists
Check if duplicates affect implicit session validation

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker constructs a session config with a blacklist containing duplicate addresses. The blacklist hash differs from the expected unique-address version, potentially causing imageHash mismatches or creating valid configs that differ from the wallet owner's intent.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Minor - primarily a correctness issue that could lead to config mismatches
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Wallets using implicit sessions with blacklists

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for duplicate blacklist entries passing sort validation

threats.title: Recovery queuedPayloadHashes Array Grows Unboundedly Enabling Storage Bloat
threats.hypothesis_description: Recovery.queuePayload appends to queuedPayloadHashes[_wallet][_signer] array without any size limit or cleanup mechanism. Each valid queue operation permanently adds an entry. While queuing requires a valid signature from the recovery signer, a compromised or malicious recovery signer could queue thousands of unique payloads (different nonces, imageHashes, etc.), bloating the contract's storage. The array is used by totalQueuedPayloads and direct index access, creating ever-growing storage that cannot be reclaimed.
threats.investigation_rationale: The push-only array pattern with no removal or max-size check creates unbounded storage growth. Each entry costs ~20k gas for a new storage slot.

proof_of_signal.pattern: queuedPayloadHashes[_wallet][_signer].push(payloadHash) with no size limit or removal

preconditions: Recovery signer key is compromised or signer is malicious
Signer can produce valid signatures for many distinct payloads

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unbounded array.push with no max length check or removal function
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Storage bloat from permanently accumulated entries
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No array size limit, no clearQueuedPayloads function, no expiry-based cleanup

analysis_questions: Is the array used in any state-changing operations that could be DoS'd?
What is the practical storage cost of bloating this array?
Can the array growth affect other Recovery contract operations?
validation_requirements: Verify array grows unboundedly with valid queuing operations
Estimate gas cost per entry and practical limits
Check if any on-chain iteration over the array exists

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Compromised recovery signer queues thousands of unique payloads (varying nonce/imageHash). Each queue costs ~50k gas. Storage bloats permanently. While this doesn't directly steal funds, it increases contract storage costs and may affect indexing/monitoring systems.
prerequisites: Access to recovery signer's private key

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Storage bloat; no direct fund loss
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Cannot clean up existing entries; must deploy new Recovery contract

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for excessive queue growth

threats.title: SessionManager Self-Call Ban Does Not Cover SessionManager Contract Address
threats.hypothesis_description: SessionManager.recoverSapientSignature bans calls to the wallet itself (call.to == wallet) but does NOT ban calls to the SessionManager contract (address(this)) for non-first-call positions in non-explicit mode. While _validateExplicitCall handles SessionManager calls at index 0, and _validateLimitUsageIncrement catches unauthorized SessionManager calls, implicit session calls have no specific SessionManager address check. An implicit session could attempt to call the SessionManager contract itself. The _validateImplicitCall would call acceptImplicitRequest on the SessionManager, which doesn't implement this interface, causing a revert. However, the blacklist check for the SessionManager address depends on the blacklist configuration.
threats.investigation_rationale: The self-call check uses `call.to == wallet` (msg.sender), but SessionManager is at a different address. If SessionManager's address is not blacklisted and an implicit session targets it, the flow depends on whether SessionManager implements ISignalsImplicitMode.
deployment_assumptions: SessionManager address not in blacklist
threats.config_evidence: SessionManager deployed separately per Deploy.s.sol; blacklist contents are config-dependent

proof_of_signal.pattern: call.to == wallet check but no call.to == address(this) check for implicit calls

preconditions: Implicit session configured without SessionManager address in blacklist
SessionManager has a function that could be called via implicit mode

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Self-call check only validates call.to != wallet, not call.to != address(this)
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Implicit sessions could target SessionManager if not blacklisted
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No explicit check for call.to == address(this) in the main loop

analysis_questions: Does SessionManager implement any functions that respond to raw calls matching ISignalsImplicitMode?
Could a future SessionManager upgrade introduce exploitable implicit-mode-compatible functions?
Does the acceptImplicitRequest revert cleanly or could it match a different function selector?
validation_requirements: Test implicit session call targeting SessionManager without blacklist
Verify acceptImplicitRequest call reverts on SessionManager
Check for selector collisions with SessionManager functions

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Implicit session signer attempts call to SessionManager address (not in blacklist). _validateImplicitCall calls ISignalsImplicitMode(SessionManager).acceptImplicitRequest which reverts since SessionManager doesn't implement it. However, if incrementUsageLimit selector collides with acceptImplicitRequest or if SessionManager is upgraded to implement it, the call could succeed.
prerequisites: SessionManager address not in implicit blacklist

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Could allow session key to manipulate usage limits via the SessionManager
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Wallets using implicit sessions without SessionManager in blacklist

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for implicit session calls targeting SessionManager address

threats.title: Static signature mode can permanently bypass nonce consumption (replay) if any static signature entry is set
threats.hypothesis_description: Calls.execute consumes nonce before signature validation, but signatureValidation can accept a 'static signature' (flag 0x80) that validates solely against `opHash` and an expiry+optional caller binding. The payload hash (opHash) in static mode includes `payload.nonce` and `payload.space`. If a wallet sets a static signature for an opHash and later reuses the same nonce value in the payload (or uses selfExecute which does not consume nonce), the static signature may allow repeated execution of the same payload without requiring signer threshold. The key risk is whether there exists any execution path that does not consume nonce but still accepts static signatures, or allows replay with the same nonce due to mis-coordination between nonce spaces or selfExecute flows.
threats.investigation_rationale: Static signatures are a powerful bypass mechanism intended for pre-approval. Any path that allows reusing the same opHash without nonce increment would be catastrophic. The code shows at least one path (selfExecute) that doesn't call _consumeNonce at all and computes opHash directly, suggesting potential replay surfaces if selfExecute can be reached indirectly or if hooks/extensions can trigger it.
deployment_assumptions: Wallet sets static signature entries via setStaticSignature for operational convenience

There exists a reachable path where the same payload can be executed without consuming nonce (e.g., via selfExecute triggered inside wallet context), or nonce space can be reset/overwritten by delegated code/hook
threats.config_evidence: Static signature usage is optional; no deployment script config indicates it is used, but BaseAuth exposes setStaticSignature (onlySelf).

proof_of_signal.lines: selfExecute() does not call _consumeNonce; execute() does
proof_of_signal.pattern: Nonce consumption absent in selfExecute; opHash computed directly; onlySelf gating depends on ability to reach self-call context

preconditions: Attacker can cause wallet to call selfExecute with an attacker-chosen payload (via any onlySelf bypass or via installed hook/extension paths)
Static signature entry exists that validates the opHash of that payload (or signatureValidation is otherwise bypassed in that path)

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: src/modules/Calls.sol selfExecute(): missing nonce consumption; src/modules/auth/BaseAuth.sol signatureValidation(): static sig early-return
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Execution path with no nonce gating combined with static signature bypass
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Same payload can be replayed indefinitely if reachable
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No nonce consumption in selfExecute; no explicit replay tracking for static signatures

analysis_questions: Can any external caller indirectly reach selfExecute (e.g., via delegatecall extension / hook / entrypoint executeUserOp)?
Is static signature intended to be usable with selfExecute, and if so how is replay prevented?
Are there any other internal-only execution entrypoints that skip nonce consumption?
validation_requirements: Trace all call paths that can invoke selfExecute in wallet context
Attempt to replay same payload multiple times under those paths in tests/fork
Confirm whether opHash includes nonce (it does) and whether nonce is enforced anywhere for selfExecute

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If an attacker can trigger wallet selfExecute for a payload whose opHash has a static signature entry, they can replay the same payload multiple times (token transfers, approvals, upgrades) since selfExecute does not increment nonces.
prerequisites: A wallet that uses static signatures

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Full wallet drain (replayed transfers/approvals), or repeated upgrades/hook installs
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Wallets using static signatures and exposing a selfExecute path (directly or via installed modules)
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Difficult; may require config change but attacker can replay until revoked

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing adversarial tests combining setStaticSignature + selfExecute reachability

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor repeated identical opHash executions if events allow correlation

threats.title: ERC-4337 validateUserOp performs external value transfer (depositTo) before signature validation, enabling griefing or reentrancy side-effects
threats.hypothesis_description: validateUserOp sends ETH to EntryPoint via `depositTo{value: missingAccountFunds}` before verifying the UserOp signature (isValidSignature). Even though EntryPoint is trusted in this model, this sequencing can still create observable side effects: (a) wallet funds can be moved to EntryPoint deposit even if signature is invalid (function returns SIG_VALIDATION_FAILED rather than reverting), causing forced locking of ETH in EntryPoint deposit; (b) if EntryPoint.depositTo has any callbacks or unexpected behavior on specific chains/implementations, it happens before validation. The typical AA pattern is to validate first, then fund.
threats.investigation_rationale: Even with trusted EntryPoint, the ordering creates a practical griefing vector if EntryPoint calls validateUserOp in simulation contexts or if bundlers can supply high missingAccountFunds values. This can force funds into deposit (not lost, but moved/locked) and may affect wallet UX and fund availability.
deployment_assumptions: entrypoint is a non-standard or modified implementation that calls validateUserOp with attacker-controlled missingAccountFunds semantics, or the bundler/entrypoint interaction can cause non-reverting invalid signatures to still move funds
Wallet holds ETH at time of validateUserOp
threats.config_evidence: Deploy.s.sol sets EntryPoint address from env or default 0x0000000071727De22E5E9d8BAf0edAc6f37da032; correctness depends on chain.

proof_of_signal.pattern: External value transfer precedes signature validation; invalid sig returns 1 not revert

preconditions: Wallet has ETH balance
EntryPoint calls validateUserOp with missingAccountFunds != 0 in cases where signature later fails

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: ETH may be moved/locked in EntryPoint deposit even when signature invalid
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check that signature is valid before funding; no cap on missingAccountFunds

analysis_questions: Does the EntryPoint ever call validateUserOp in a way where an invalid signature still results in moved funds?
Is missingAccountFunds bounded by EntryPoint logic to available shortfall only?
Can this be used to force-lock ETH in EntryPoint and disrupt wallet operations?
validation_requirements: Simulate validateUserOp with missingAccountFunds > 0 and invalid signature; observe whether deposit increases
Review target chain EntryPoint implementation behavior
Confirm whether wallet can withdraw EntryPoint deposits (interface not present here)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker influences AA flow so validateUserOp is invoked with nonzero missingAccountFunds but invalid signature; wallet deposits ETH to EntryPoint and validation fails, locking funds in deposit and reducing wallet liquid ETH.
prerequisites: AA flow usage and non-standard EntryPoint/bundler behavior

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Funds not necessarily lost but moved/locked; can disrupt payment of calls requiring on-wallet ETH
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Wallets using ERC-4337 with low ETH balances
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Likely recoverable via EntryPoint withdraw mechanisms (not shown in scope)

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test asserting signature validation happens before funding side-effects

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: compare deposit deltas for failed validation ops

threats.title: Passkeys compact signature decoding uses unchecked slicing without length validation, enabling revert-based DoS on sapient verification paths
threats.hypothesis_description: Passkeys._decodeSignature parses `_signature` using multiple LibBytes reads and slices (`_signature[pointer:nextPointer]`) inside an `unchecked` block, without validating that `_signature` is long enough for each field. If used as a signer inside BaseSig (sapient compact leaf), malformed signatures can trigger out-of-bounds slices and revert, causing the overall wallet signature validation to revert. This creates a DoS vector when a wallet configuration includes Passkeys as a signer and an attacker can submit malformed signatures to `Calls.execute` or `isValidSignature` verification endpoints.
threats.investigation_rationale: While attackers still need to present a merkle path consistent with config to pass auth, revert-based DoS is realistic when `isValidSignature` is used by third-party protocols (e.g., order validation) and can be spammed. The lack of graceful failure in parsing is a common cause of protocol-level DoS.
deployment_assumptions: Wallet signer tree includes Passkeys as a SapientCompact signer leaf
External systems call wallet.isValidSignature and treat reverts as failure/DoS rather than false
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence indicates Passkeys is deployed/used; Passkeys contract exists and is intended as sapient signer.

proof_of_signal.lines: _decodeSignature(): unchecked pointer arithmetic and calldata slicing without bounds checks
proof_of_signal.pattern: Potential out-of-bounds slice revert on malformed signature

preconditions: Passkeys signer is reachable in BaseSig recovery for the attempted operation
Attacker can submit malformed passkey signature bytes

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unchecked pointer arithmetic + slicing on calldata
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Reverts instead of returning invalid signature result
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No explicit length guards before slices/reads

analysis_questions: Does BaseSig treat sapient call reverts as a revert of the entire recoverBranch (it will, since it's a direct call)?
Is passkey signer intended to be used in high-frequency 1271 validations where revert DoS matters?
Can malformed signature reach Passkeys call without already failing merkle root checks earlier?
validation_requirements: Fuzz Passkeys.recoverSapientSignatureCompact with short signatures and observe revert conditions
Integrate Passkeys as signer in BaseSig tree and confirm Calls.execute reverts during signature recovery
Assess mitigations: try/catch around sapient calls (not present in BaseSig)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker spams calls to wallet.isValidSignature with signatures that route into Passkeys leaf and trigger revert during decoding, causing dependent protocols to fail validation or waste gas.
prerequisites: Victim protocol treats revert as hard failure

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect: gas griefing / operational disruption rather than fund theft
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Wallets using Passkeys signer and integrators calling isValidSignature
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Use robust off-chain validation / catch reverts; change signer config

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing negative tests for malformed compact passkey signatures

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor revert reasons from Passkeys.InvalidPasskeySignature vs generic slice panics

threats.title: Guest Contract Allows Unauthorized ETH Drain by Any Caller
threats.hypothesis_description: The Guest contract's fallback function decodes and executes arbitrary calls from msg.data without any authentication, signature validation, or nonce protection. Any ETH sent to the Guest contract (accidentally or intentionally) can be immediately drained by anyone by calling the Guest with a packed payload that transfers the ETH balance to the attacker.
threats.investigation_rationale: Guest is deployed as a singleton (see Deploy.s.sol). If any user or contract accidentally sends ETH to the Guest address (e.g., wrong address, misconfigured integration), anyone can drain it. The Guest has a receive() function (inherited from Hooks? No - Guest doesn't inherit Hooks. But it has a fallback that's payable implicitly via Payload execution). Actually, the fallback itself is `payable` and LibOptim.call forwards value. If ETH arrives at Guest via any means, any caller can execute arbitrary calls including value transfers.
threats.config_evidence: Deploy.s.sol deploys Guest as a singleton contract at a deterministic address

proof_of_signal.pattern: fallback() external payable decodes msg.data as packed calls and executes them without any authentication or signature check

preconditions: Guest contract holds ETH (sent accidentally or through misconfigured interaction)
Attacker knows the Guest contract address

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No authentication: anyone can call Guest fallback with arbitrary packed payload
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Craft packed payload with call to attacker address transferring full Guest ETH balance
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: 100% of ETH held by Guest contract

economic_questions: Is Guest expected to hold ETH in any legitimate workflow?
Can the Guest receive ETH through normal fallback (no explicit receive)?
Are there integrations that might mistakenly send ETH to Guest?
validation_requirements: Send ETH to Guest, then call fallback with drain payload
Verify no access controls exist
Check if Guest contract can receive ETH via direct transfer

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All ETH held by Guest contract

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after ETH is sent to Guest

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Anyone who accidentally sends ETH to the Guest contract
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Any ETH sent to Guest address

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check Guest's ability to receive ETH (payable fallback)
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify packed payload can encode ETH transfer to arbitrary address

threats.title: Recovery Payloads Cannot Be Cancelled, Creating Irreversible Time-Bomb After Key Compromise
threats.hypothesis_description: Recovery.queuePayload stores payload hashes with their queuing timestamp, but provides no mechanism to cancel queued payloads. Once a recovery signer's key is compromised and used to queue a malicious config update, the payload becomes permanently pending. The wallet owner must update the wallet configuration to invalidate the recovery signer before the timelock expires. If the owner fails to act within the requiredDeltaTime window, the attacker can execute the recovery and take over the wallet.
threats.investigation_rationale: The write-once nature of timestampForQueuedPayload (checked via AlreadyQueued) and the absence of any cancellation function create a permanent attack surface. The queuedPayloadHashes array also grows unboundedly, and there's no garbage collection. The requiredDeltaTime is stored as uint24 (max ~194 days), limiting the protection window.

proof_of_signal.pattern: queuePayload stores timestamp permanently; no cancel/remove function exists; AlreadyQueued prevents re-queuing but not cancellation

preconditions: Wallet uses Recovery module as a sapient signer
Recovery signer's private key is compromised
Attacker queues a malicious config update payload
Wallet owner does not update config before timelock expires

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: timestampForQueuedPayload set once, never cleared; no cancel function
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Queue malicious payload, wait for timelock, execute recovery to take over wallet
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Full wallet takeover after timelock expiry if owner doesn't respond

economic_questions: What is the typical requiredDeltaTime for production recovery configs?
Can the wallet owner reliably detect and respond to queued payloads?
Is the unbounded queuedPayloadHashes array a DoS vector?
validation_requirements: Verify no cancellation path exists in Recovery.sol
Test that config update before timelock invalidates the recovery
Assess gas cost of unbounded array growth

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All wallet assets if owner doesn't respond in time

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When wallet owner is likely unavailable (holidays, weekends)

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Wallet owners with compromised recovery signers
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All assets in wallets using Recovery module

threats.title: LibBytes Unchecked Reads Enable Silent Signature/Payload Misparse Across Multiple Contexts
threats.hypothesis_description: LibBytes functions (readUint8, readAddress, readBytes32, etc.) use raw calldataload without bounds checking. When reading beyond the actual data slice, calldataload returns zero-padded values. This affects multiple contexts: (1) BaseSig.recoverBranch parsing truncated signatures may produce zero addresses/weights, (2) Payload.fromPackedCalls parsing truncated payloads may produce calls to address(0) with zero values, (3) SessionSig.recoverConfiguration parsing may produce malformed configurations. Any of these could lead to unexpected behavior.
threats.investigation_rationale: The absence of bounds checking is documented in LibBytes but creates systemic risk across all consumers. A truncated signature could be parsed with zero-filled values that coincidentally match a valid configuration element. The impact depends on downstream validation catching these zero values, which is inconsistent across different code paths.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Documentation states: 'These functions do not check if the input index is within the bounds of the data array. Reading out of bounds may return dirty values.'

preconditions: Attacker crafts a truncated signature or payload
Zero-filled values from OOB reads produce valid-looking data downstream
Downstream checks don't catch all zero-value edge cases

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: calldataload beyond slice returns 0; parsed as address(0), weight 0, size 0, etc.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Submit truncated signatures that parse with zero-padded values to bypass validation
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Potential signature forgery or payload manipulation depending on config

economic_questions: Which code paths are most vulnerable to zero-filled OOB reads?
Can a truncated signature produce a valid imageHash?
Can a truncated payload produce exploitable calls to address(0)?
validation_requirements: Fuzz BaseSig.recover with truncated signatures, check for unexpected valid parses
Test Payload.fromPackedCalls with truncated inputs
Map all LibBytes consumers and their zero-value handling

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Varies - could be full wallet compromise in worst case

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Map all calldataload sites vs data length checks
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Systematic testing of truncated inputs across all parsers

threats.title: Session Configuration Parsing Reverts for Valid Permissions Without Rules
threats.hypothesis_description: Session configurations containing multiple permissions with zero rules will cause an out-of-bounds array write, rendering the session key unusable.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `recoverConfiguration` function estimates the maximum number of permissions by dividing the encoded length by `MIN_ENCODED_PERMISSION_SIZE` (94 bytes). However, a permission with zero rules (restricting only the target address) takes only 21 bytes. If a user configures multiple such permissions, the estimated array size will be smaller than the actual number of permissions, causing a Solidity out-of-bounds revert when writing to the array.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 maxPermissionsSize = encoded.length / MIN_ENCODED_PERMISSION_SIZE;

preconditions: User creates a session configuration with multiple permissions that have 0 rules
The total encoded length divided by 94 is less than the actual number of permissions

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Valid configurations naturally trigger the under-allocation
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Breaks session key functionality, causing failed integrations or lost execution opportunities for automated trading bots using session keys

economic_questions: How common are target-only permissions in real-world usage?
Can this be used to grief other users if session configs are shared?

Attempt to recover the signature and observe the revert

preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Permanent for the affected session key

threats.title: Session Keys Must Include Dummy Usage Increments for Previously Used Limits
threats.hypothesis_description: Session keys that have previously consumed cumulative limits are forced to include dummy increment calls in all future transactions, even if the current transaction does not consume those limits.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `_validateLimitUsageIncrement` function checks if `totalValueUsed > 0` (which is initialized from storage). If true, it requires the first call in the payload to be an `incrementUsageLimit` call matching that exact amount. This means if a session key spends 1 ETH in tx 1, tx 2 (even if it spends 0 ETH) must include a dummy increment call for 1 ETH, otherwise the transaction reverts.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (sessionUsageLimits[i].totalValueUsed > 0) { limits[limitIndex++] = ... }

preconditions: Session key has previously executed a transaction that consumed a cumulative limit or native value limit

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: totalValueUsed initialized from storage, forcing increment requirement
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Strict validation logic rejects valid payloads that lack the dummy increment
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Causes unexpected transaction failures for automated systems, leading to lost MEV or arbitrage opportunities

economic_questions: Does the SDK automatically append these dummy increments?

threats.title: Updating Session Permissions Resets Cumulative Usage Limits Allowing Limit Bypass
threats.hypothesis_description: When a wallet owner updates a session key's permissions (e.g., increasing a limit), the usage hash changes, resetting the session key's accumulated usage to zero and allowing it to spend more than intended.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `usageHash` for cumulative rules is computed as `keccak256(abi.encode(signer, permission, i))`. Because the entire `permission` struct is included in the hash, any change to the rule (like increasing `rule.value`) results in a completely new hash. The previous usage stored under the old hash is orphaned, and the session key starts from 0 usage against the new limit.

proof_of_signal.pattern: bytes32 usageHash = keccak256(abi.encode(signer, permission, i));

preconditions: Session key has consumed some of its cumulative limit
Wallet owner updates the session configuration to modify the permission rule

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: usageHash includes the mutable permission struct
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Owner action inadvertently resets the counter
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: A compromised session key can extract `old_limit + new_limit` instead of just `new_limit`, stealing more funds than the owner authorized.

threats.title: Passkey Signatures Fail on Chains Without P256 Verifiers
threats.hypothesis_description: The Passkeys module relies on a hardcoded Solidity verifier address and the RIP-7212 precompile. On chains where neither is present, passkey signatures will systematically revert, locking users out of their wallets.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `P256.verifySignature` library attempts to use the RIP-7212 precompile at `0x100`. If it fails, it falls back to a hardcoded Solidity verifier at `0x000000000000D01eA45F9eFD5c54f037Fa57Ea1a`. If a wallet is deployed on an L2 or alt-chain that has not implemented RIP-7212 and where the Sequence team (or others) has not deployed the Solidity verifier at that exact deterministic address, all passkey validations will revert with `P256VerificationFailed`.
deployment_assumptions: Target chain lacks RIP-7212 precompile
Solidity verifier is not deployed at the hardcoded address on the target chain
threats.config_evidence: P256.sol lines 28-33 define the hardcoded addresses.

proof_of_signal.pattern: address internal constant VERIFIER = 0x000000000000D01eA45F9eFD5c54f037Fa57Ea1a;

preconditions: Wallet uses Passkeys as the primary or sole signer
Deployed on a chain missing both the precompile and the specific verifier contract

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Hardcoded external contract dependencies for core cryptography
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Users lose access to their funds permanently if they have no alternative signers configured.

threats.title: Chained Signatures Fail for Nested Wallets Due to Missing parentWallets Propagation
threats.hypothesis_description: Chained signatures fail to propagate the `parentWallets` array to intermediate configuration update payloads, causing signature verification to fail for nested wallet setups.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `BaseSig.recoverChained`, the `linkedPayload` used for intermediate signature verification is initialized with `kind = KIND_CONFIG_UPDATE` but the `parentWallets` array is left empty. When `_payload.hash()` is called, it computes the EIP-712 hash using an empty `walletsHash`. If the original signer signed the config update expecting the actual `parentWallets` array (which is required for nested signers to bind the signature to the correct context), the hashes will mismatch and the chained signature will revert.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Payload.Decoded memory linkedPayload; linkedPayload.kind = Payload.KIND_CONFIG_UPDATE;

preconditions: Wallet is configured as a nested signer (uses `parentWallets`)
User attempts to use a chained signature to migrate configurations

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: EIP-712 hash mismatch due to empty array
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Nested wallets cannot use chained signatures to execute transactions across configuration updates, potentially locking funds if old signers are lost.

economic_questions: Can the user intentionally sign with an empty `parentWallets` to bypass this?
Does signing with an empty `parentWallets` expose the signature to cross-wallet replay attacks?

threats.title: Recovery ecrecover address(0) Allows Unauthorized Payload Queuing
threats.hypothesis_description: Recovery.isValidSignature does not check for ecrecover returning address(0). When _signer is address(0) and a 64-byte invalid signature is provided, ecrecover returns address(0), and addr == _signer evaluates to true. This allows anyone to queue recovery payloads for any wallet using _signer = address(0). If the wallet's recovery configuration includes address(0) as a recovery leaf, the queued payload becomes executable after the timelock.
threats.investigation_rationale: The Recovery contract's signature validation has the same ecrecover address(0) issue as BaseSig. Combined with a recovery config that includes address(0), an attacker can queue malicious config update payloads without any valid key.

proof_of_signal.pattern: address addr = ecrecover(rPayloadHash, v, r, s); if (addr == _signer) { return true; } — no check for addr == address(0); when _signer is address(0), any invalid 64-byte signature passes

preconditions: Wallet has Recovery contract as a sapient signer in its configuration
Recovery configuration includes address(0) as a recovery signer leaf
Attacker calls queuePayload with _signer = address(0)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: queuePayload called with _signer = address(0) and any 64-byte invalid signature succeeds
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires recovery config with address(0) as signer

How many deployed wallets might have recovery configs vulnerable to this?

threats.title: Static Signature Caller Restriction Fails When Used Via ERC4337 Path
threats.hypothesis_description: In BaseAuth.signatureValidation, static signatures with a non-zero caller restriction check addr != msg.sender. When validateUserOp calls this.isValidSignature(userOpHash, userOp.signature), the msg.sender for isValidSignature becomes address(this) (the wallet itself), not the original EntryPoint or the UserOperation sender. A static signature restricted to a specific external caller (e.g., a relayer address) would fail validation via the ERC4337 path because msg.sender is the wallet, not the intended caller.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a functional inconsistency between direct signature validation and ERC4337-mediated validation. Static signatures with caller restrictions cannot be used via ERC4337, potentially causing unexpected transaction failures.

proof_of_signal.pattern: this.isValidSignature(userOpHash, userOp.signature) — external self-call changes msg.sender to address(this), breaking static signature caller checks

preconditions: Wallet has a static signature set with a non-zero caller address restriction
Transaction is submitted via ERC4337 EntryPoint (validateUserOp path)

threats.title: Session Call Hash Loses Chain Binding When noChainId Is Set, Enabling Cross-Chain Replay
threats.hypothesis_description: SessionSig.hashCallWithReplayProtection uses payload.noChainId to determine whether to include block.chainid in the per-call hash. When noChainId is true, the hash uses 0 instead of the actual chain ID. Since the session key signs per-call hashes, and noChainId is set by the outer signature flag (not controlled by the session key), session call signatures become replayable across chains. An attacker who captures a session transaction on one chain can replay it on another chain where the same wallet exists with the same session configuration.
threats.investigation_rationale: The noChainId flag was designed for cross-chain wallet operations but creates replay risk for session-bounded calls. Session keys are typically granted limited permissions, and cross-chain replay could allow unauthorized operations on chains the session key was not intended to operate on.

proof_of_signal.pattern: payload.noChainId ? 0 : block.chainid — when noChainId is true, session call hash excludes chainid, enabling cross-chain replay of session signatures

preconditions: Wallet uses session keys with the SessionManager
Outer signature uses noChainId = true
Same wallet address exists on multiple chains with same session configuration
Same nonce space and nonce value are available on both chains

threats.title: Hooks Fallback Delegates to Registered Hook Without Caller Authentication
threats.hypothesis_description: The Hooks.fallback function performs a delegatecall to the registered hook implementation for any matching selector, regardless of who the caller is. Any external address can trigger hook execution by calling the wallet with a matching function selector. The hook runs in the wallet's storage context with full write access. If a hook implementation does not implement its own caller authentication, any external party can trigger state changes in the wallet's storage through the hook.
threats.investigation_rationale: While hook installation requires onlySelf authorization, the actual invocation has no access control. This creates a trust boundary violation where the hook's security depends entirely on the hook implementation's own access control, which is not enforced or verified by the wallet.

proof_of_signal.pattern: fallback() external payable { ... (bool success, bytes memory result) = target.delegatecall(msg.data); ... } — no msg.sender check before delegatecall; any caller triggers hook execution in wallet context

preconditions: Wallet has at least one hook registered via addHook
Hook implementation does not enforce msg.sender access control
Attacker knows the registered hook selector

initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Wallet owner must have installed a vulnerable hook

threats.title: SessionManager Allows incrementUsageLimit Call at Index 0 Without Full Permission Validation
threats.hypothesis_description: In ExplicitSessionManager._validateExplicitCall, when call.to == address(this) (SessionManager) and callIdx == 0, the function returns early without validating any permission. The function only checks that value is 0 and returns the unchanged sessionUsageLimits. While _validateLimitUsageIncrement later validates the call data hash matches expected increments, the initial permission bypass means the first call to SessionManager is not checked against any signer permission configuration. Combined with the fact that incrementUsageLimit is externally callable and only enforces monotonic increase, the overall limit tracking depends entirely on the data hash match.
threats.investigation_rationale: The permission validation skip for self-calls at index 0 creates a control flow gap. If the data hash validation in _validateLimitUsageIncrement has any weakness or if the expected data construction diverges from actual execution, usage limits could be manipulated.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (call.to == address(this)) { if (callIdx != 0) revert; if (call.value > 0) revert; return sessionUsageLimits; } — returns without permission validation for self-call at index 0

preconditions: Session key has any explicit permissions configured
First call in payload targets the SessionManager contract

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: First call to SessionManager skips all permission checks; relies solely on data hash match for integrity
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — any session key holder can structure payload this way
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any session transaction with usage limits

operational_questions: Is the data hash match in _validateLimitUsageIncrement sufficient to prevent manipulation?

threats.title: Storage-slot collision / corruption risk due to using `sstore(address(), _imp)` as implementation slot in proxy
threats.hypothesis_description: The proxy uses a non-standard storage slot for implementation: `sstore(address(), _imp)` (slot key equals the wallet's own address value). Any delegated code (hooks, extensions, future modules) that writes to storage slot `bytes32(uint256(uint160(walletAddress)))` will overwrite the implementation pointer, potentially bricking the wallet or redirecting execution. Even without a malicious hook, an innocuous module with an overlapping storage layout/slot computation could cause corruption. A malicious hook (once installed) can deterministically seize control by writing this slot.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a real operational hazard for an extensible wallet platform: new modules/hook implementations can inadvertently collide with the implementation slot, causing catastrophic failures. The exploitability becomes HIGH if any path allows untrusted delegatecall code (hooks) to run.
deployment_assumptions: Wallets may install third-party hooks/extensions that are not formally verified against this proxy slot convention
Developers/operators may add new modules over time that write to computed slots
threats.config_evidence: phase-a-architecture-analysis.json inventories.upgradeability_surface notes 'collision_risk: high' for Wallet.sol proxy slot scheme; no mitigation code found in Implementation.sol

proof_of_signal.pattern: _setImplementation uses `sstore(address(), _imp)` and `_getImplementation` uses `sload(address())`

preconditions: A hook/extension is installed (onlySelf) that executes via delegatecall in wallet context, OR a future module writes to the colliding slot unintentionally
Hook/extension contains a write to the proxy implementation slot (intentionally or accidentally)

threats.title: Relayer Gas Manipulation Allows Selective Call Griefing in Batches
threats.hypothesis_description: The Calls.execute function allows users to specify a gasLimit for individual calls. A relayer can manipulate the transaction's overall gas limit to provide exactly enough gas to pass the gasleft() < gasLimit check, but not enough for the call to succeed. If the call uses BEHAVIOR_IGNORE_ERROR, it fails silently while the rest of the batch (including relayer fee payments) succeeds.
threats.investigation_rationale: Relayers have control over the exact gas provided to a transaction. If they can force a user's primary action to fail while still collecting their fee, it creates a misaligned economic incentive.

proof_of_signal.pattern: gasleft() < gasLimit check followed by LibOptim.call which can fail due to 63/64 gas rule

preconditions: User submits a batch via a relayer
Batch includes a fee payment call and a target call with BEHAVIOR_IGNORE_ERROR
Target call has a specific gasLimit set

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Relayer restricts outer gas so gasleft() >= gasLimit passes, but the 63/64th gas forwarded is insufficient for the sub-call
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Relayer collects fee without executing the user's expensive computation, saving gas costs and profiting the difference

economic_questions: Do users commonly use BEHAVIOR_IGNORE_ERROR for critical calls?
What is the exact gas margin a relayer can exploit using the 63/64 rule?
validation_requirements: Construct a payload with a fee transfer and a high-gas target call

threats.title: Session Usage Limit Wasting via IGNORE_ERROR Behavior
threats.hypothesis_description: Session keys are subject to cumulative usage limits. These limits are incremented based on the payload's intended actions before execution begins. If a session key submits a payload where calls use BEHAVIOR_IGNORE_ERROR and those calls fail, the usage limits are still permanently consumed, allowing a relayer to drain a session key's limits without executing actions.
threats.investigation_rationale: The separation of limit incrementing (first call) and actual execution (subsequent calls) means failures in execution still cost the user their limits. This can be exploited for griefing.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Usage limits are validated and incremented via the first call, regardless of subsequent call success

preconditions: Session key has strict usage limits
Session key submits a batch with BEHAVIOR_IGNORE_ERROR calls
Relayer or external conditions cause the calls to fail

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Limits incremented before execution; no refund mechanism if execution fails
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Relayer manipulates gas to fail the call, consuming the session key's limits
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit for attacker, but causes DoS for the session key holder, potentially preventing timely actions (e.g., collateral top-ups)

economic_questions: Can this be used to prevent a session key from executing a critical time-sensitive action?
Is there any way for the session key to recover lost limits?
validation_requirements: Execute a payload where the first call (increment) succeeds but subsequent calls fail
Verify that limits are permanently consumed

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas cost of the failed transaction
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect (e.g., profiting from a liquidation the session key couldn't prevent)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When the session key needs to execute a critical action near their limit boundary

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of session privileges until limits are reset by owner

threats.title: Cumulative Permission Rule Usage Never Persisted to Storage
threats.hypothesis_description: In PermissionValidator.validatePermission, when cumulative rules are processed, the computed cumulative usage amount is assigned to a local memory variable (`usageLimit.usageAmount = value256`) but this update never propagates back to the `newUsageLimits` array. Solidity memory struct assignment creates copies, so after `newUsageLimits[j] = usageLimit` the array entry and local variable are independent. The returned array always contains `usageAmount = 0` for cumulative entries. This flows to `_validateLimitUsageIncrement` which constructs the expected `incrementUsageLimit` call with `usageAmount = 0`. Consequently, `incrementUsageLimit` writes 0 to storage, meaning cumulative limits are never actually recorded. A session key with a cumulative spending limit of X can spend up to X per transaction, indefinitely across unlimited transactions.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a code-level memory semantics bug that completely defeats cumulative permission enforcement. Session keys designed to have lifetime spending caps become uncapped. The bug is deterministic and requires no special conditions beyond a session key with cumulative rules.

proof_of_signal.pattern: usageLimit = UsageLimit({...}); newUsageLimits[j] = usageLimit; ... usageLimit.usageAmount = value256; // local copy only, array not updated

preconditions: Wallet has session key configured with cumulative permission rules
Session key holder wants to exceed cumulative limit
At least one cumulative ParameterRule exists in the Permission

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: newUsageLimits[j] = usageLimit (copy); later usageLimit.usageAmount = value256 (updates local, not array)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Each transaction independently reads storage (always 0), validates against rule.value, and writes 0 back to storage via incrementUsageLimit
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Session key with cumulative limit of 1 ETH can transfer up to 1 ETH per transaction, unlimited total. With 100 txs = 100 ETH stolen

economic_questions: What is the typical cumulative limit configured for session keys in production?
How quickly can a session key submit multiple transactions before detection?
Are there other rate-limiting mechanisms outside the smart contract?
validation_requirements: Construct a test showing storage remains 0 after incrementUsageLimit with cumulative rules
Execute two sequential transactions both using the full cumulative limit
Verify the usageAmount in returned newUsageLimits array is always 0

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: 0 (session key holder uses their existing session permissions)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas costs per transaction (~0.001 ETH per tx on L2)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to entire wallet balance, limited only by per-call rule.value
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Multiple transactions, each within a few seconds

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time while session key is valid

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Wallet owners who grant session keys with cumulative limits
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Full value of assets accessible within per-call limits
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All assets held by wallets using cumulative session permissions

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace usageAmount value from validatePermission return through _validateLimitUsageIncrement to incrementUsageLimit

threats.title: Static Signature Permanent Pre-Authorization Enables Persistent Access After Signer Removal
threats.hypothesis_description: BaseAuth.setStaticSignature allows the wallet to pre-authorize an opHash with an optional caller restriction and expiry timestamp. The static signature is stored in a mapping independent of the wallet's imageHash/signer configuration. If a wallet sets a static signature for a config-update or transaction opHash, and later updates its signer configuration (removing a signer), the static signature remains valid. A removed signer who knows about a pre-authorized static signature could use it even after being removed from the config, as long as the timestamp hasn't expired and the caller restriction matches.
threats.investigation_rationale: Static signatures bypass the normal signature validation entirely (checked before imageHash recovery). They persist in storage independently of configuration changes, creating a potential backdoor if not properly managed.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Static signature stored in independent mapping from imageHash; signatureValidation checks static sig first, bypassing imageHash/weight validation

preconditions: Wallet has set a static signature for a specific opHash
Static signature has future expiry timestamp
Wallet configuration changes (signer removed) but static signature not cleared
Attacker knows the pre-authorized opHash

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Static signature validity: timestamp > block.timestamp AND (addr == 0 || addr == msg.sender)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Previously authorized entity retains execution rights via uncleaned static signature after config update
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Execute pre-authorized transaction (could be arbitrary call payload) after being removed as signer

economic_questions: How commonly are static signatures used in production?
Are static signatures automatically cleared during config updates?
What types of operations are typically pre-authorized?
validation_requirements: Set static signature, update config to remove signer, verify static sig still works
Verify that only the specific opHash is executable (not arbitrary operations)
Check if config update flows include static signature cleanup

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Wallet owner who removed signer but didn't clear static signatures

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Examine typical static signature usage patterns and expiry durations
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify static sig persistence across config changes

threats.title: Factory allows deploying wallets with arbitrary mainModule, enabling address-squatting/impersonation at deterministic CREATE2 addresses
threats.hypothesis_description: Factory.deploy is permissionless and accepts any `_mainModule`. A third party can deploy a wallet proxy at a deterministic CREATE2 address for a given `_salt` but with a malicious implementation, potentially confusing integrators/users who assume a salt corresponds to a specific module (e.g., Stage1Module) or who only compute address from (factory,salt) without including the module in address derivation. This can be used to impersonate a wallet address in off-chain systems that derive or cache addresses incorrectly.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a realistic operational integration failure: many systems derive counterfactual addresses with incomplete parameters. The on-chain code permits such deployments, so correctness must be enforced operationally and in SDKs.
deployment_assumptions: Some integrators/users derive wallet addresses as `create2(factory, salt)` without binding to `_mainModule` (or treat salt as globally unique across modules).
Attackers can choose salts that victims intend to use.
threats.config_evidence: Factory.deploy(address _mainModule, bytes32 _salt) includes `_mainModule` in init code, but CREATE2 address derivation depends on init code hash; off-chain derivation mistakes are plausible. No enforcement in Factory.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Permissionless create2 deploy with caller-chosen module + salt

preconditions: Victim relies on off-chain address derivation/caching that is not module-specific
Attacker can deploy first using victim’s intended salt with a different module

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Users may share salts (e.g., imageHash-based salts) and assume uniqueness; attacker can front-run deployments conceptually without mempool by simply deploying earlier in time.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Before a victim deploys their intended wallet

operational_questions: How does the official SDK derive counterfactual addresses (does it bind module address)?
Do any partner integrations assume a single global factory+salt mapping?
Is `_salt` always the imageHash (as docs imply), and does Stage1Auth validate imageHash against address(this) to prevent misuse after deployment?

Create test: deploy with malicious module using same salt, check whether any off-chain tooling misidentifies it

threats.title: Nonce Overflow at uint256 Max Enables Complete Transaction Replay
threats.hypothesis_description: In Nonce._consumeNonce, the nonce increment uses unchecked arithmetic: unchecked { uint256 newNonce = _nonce + 1; }. If a nonce in any space reaches type(uint256).max, the next increment wraps to 0, resetting the nonce counter. This allows replay of ALL previously executed transactions in that nonce space, since every historical nonce value becomes valid again.
threats.investigation_rationale: The unchecked block explicitly skips overflow protection. While reaching type(uint256).max requires ~1.16e77 transactions (practically impossible with sequential nonces), the code contains a demonstrable correctness flaw that violates the nonce monotonicity invariant.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unchecked { uint256 newNonce = _nonce + 1; } wraps at type(uint256).max

preconditions: A nonce space reaches type(uint256).max (practically infeasible with sequential increments)

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check for nonce < type(uint256).max before increment

analysis_questions: Is there any mechanism that could advance nonces faster than sequential execution?
Could the nonce be manually set via storage manipulation (e.g., via hooks)?
What is the actual gas cost to reach max nonce?
validation_requirements: Verify nonce wraps to 0 at max via unit test
Check if any code path allows non-sequential nonce advancement
Assess if hook delegatecall could write to nonce storage directly

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If nonce reaches max (via normal operations or storage manipulation via malicious hook), next execution wraps nonce to 0. All historical signed transactions for that space become replayable.
prerequisites: Nonce must reach type(uint256).max in a specific space

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete replay of all historical transactions in the affected nonce space
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: The specific wallet with overflowed nonce
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Switch to a different nonce space

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for nonce overflow behavior at uint256 max

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: None if nonce can be advanced via other means
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring nonce values approaching max

threats.title: Hooks Fallback Delegatecall Enables Arbitrary Storage Manipulation by Malicious Hook
threats.hypothesis_description: Hooks.sol fallback performs delegatecall to hook implementations with full msg.data. The hook executes in the wallet's storage context with no restrictions on storage access. A malicious hook can overwrite the implementation slot (sstore(address(), attacker_impl)), the imageHash (IMAGE_HASH_KEY), nonce values, reentrancy guard, or any other storage slot. This grants total wallet control. While hook installation requires onlySelf authorization, a wallet owner who installs a seemingly benign hook with a hidden backdoor would lose all assets.
threats.investigation_rationale: The fallback at Hooks.sol delegates to any registered hook with no storage access restrictions. The delegatecall grants the hook the same privileges as the wallet implementation itself.

proof_of_signal.pattern: delegatecall(msg.data) to registered hook with no storage access restrictions

preconditions: Wallet owner installs a malicious or compromised hook via addHook
External caller invokes the hook's selector on the wallet

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: (bool success, bytes memory result) = target.delegatecall(msg.data); with target from storage
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Hook has unrestricted storage write access in wallet context
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No storage slot restrictions, no immutable slot protection, no hook sandboxing

analysis_questions: Can a hook overwrite the implementation slot to hijack the proxy?
Can a hook modify IMAGE_HASH_KEY to change signer configuration?
Is there any hook verification or auditing mechanism?
validation_requirements: Create a malicious hook that overwrites the implementation slot
Demonstrate wallet takeover via hook-modified storage
Verify no storage protection exists for critical slots

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Wallet owner installs hook with selector 0xdeadbeef. Hook contract contains: function handleDeadbeef() { assembly { sstore(address(), attacker_implementation) } }. Attacker calls wallet with selector 0xdeadbeef. Hook executes via delegatecall, overwrites implementation. All future calls to wallet are handled by attacker's implementation.
prerequisites: Wallet owner must install the malicious hook via onlySelf

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete wallet takeover - all assets drainable
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: The specific wallet with malicious hook installed

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying hooks cannot modify critical storage slots

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: DefinedHook event emitted at installation; delegatecall storage writes not logged

threats.title: Wallet implementation storage slot collision risk: Implementation pointer stored at slot keyed by address() may be clobbered by delegatecalled hooks/extensions
threats.hypothesis_description: Implementation._setImplementation stores the proxy's implementation address at storage slot `sstore(address(), _imp)` (i.e., slot == uint256(uint160(address(this))) in the proxy context). Any delegatecall-executed code (Hooks.fallback hook implementations, Calls delegateCall extensions) can write to arbitrary storage including this slot, changing the wallet implementation and taking over execution permanently. While installing hooks is onlySelf (intended privileged), this storage layout is unusually collision-prone: any module using Solidity state variables could accidentally map to this slot under certain compiler layouts, and any mistakenly-installed extension/hook can brick or hijack the wallet.
threats.investigation_rationale: Phase A notes high collision risk due to non-EIP1967 slot choice. Even without a malicious actor, accidental collisions during future upgrades/modules are realistic. With a malicious hook, takeover is trivial; realism is low because it requires privileged installation, but severity remains high.
deployment_assumptions: Wallet installs third-party hook/extension code via addHook or executes delegateCall=true calls
Hook/extension contains storage writes (intentional or accidental) that can reach slot `address()`
threats.config_evidence: Hooks system is enabled by default in Stage1Module/Stage2Module; addHook is onlySelf. No further allowlist.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Non-standard impl slot equals address(); delegatecall-capable system allows arbitrary storage writes

preconditions: Wallet executes delegatecall into untrusted or buggy code (hook or extension)
That code writes to the implementation slot

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Implementation pointer stored in a non-reserved, collision-prone slot
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Accidental or malicious overwrite of implementation causes permanent takeover/brick
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No EIP-1967 slot; no module sandboxing; no rollback protection

analysis_questions: Are there any shipped hook implementations/extensions that could collide with this slot unintentionally?
Do any delegatecall paths exist besides Hooks.fallback and Calls delegateCall that are reachable in normal operation?
Is there an emergency recovery path if implementation slot is corrupted?
validation_requirements: Compute actual slot index for implementation in proxy context and attempt overwrite via a minimal hook
Review planned/known extensions for storage layout assumptions
Check if updateImplementation can recover from overwrite (requires onlySelf)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User installs a third-party hook; hook delegatecall writes to slot `address()` and changes implementation to attacker-controlled logic, draining assets on next call.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Often none if implementation changed to hostile code; depends on attacker behavior

threats.test_coverage_gap: No regression tests asserting implementation slot is protected from common storage layouts

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: watch for unexpected ImplementationUpdated events / implementation slot diffs

threats.title: Nonce Unchecked Overflow Allows Theoretical Replay After type(uint256).max Transactions
threats.hypothesis_description: Nonce._consumeNonce uses an unchecked block for nonce increment: `unchecked { uint256 newNonce = _nonce + 1; }`. At type(uint256).max, the nonce wraps to 0, enabling replay of every previously executed transaction in that nonce space. While reaching this limit requires 2^256 transactions (computationally impossible), the code defect violates the stated invariant of strict sequential nonce advancement.
threats.investigation_rationale: The nonce overflow violates INV-STATE-003 (nonce must strictly increment) and INV-ECON-003 (nonce conservation). Though impractical, this represents a correctness defect. A simple `require(_nonce < type(uint256).max)` check would eliminate the theoretical risk at negligible gas cost.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unchecked { uint256 newNonce = _nonce + 1; } wraps at max uint256

preconditions: Nonce in a specific space reaches type(uint256).max (computationally impossible)
Attacker has stored valid signed transactions from nonce 0 onwards

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: uint256 newNonce = _nonce + 1 in unchecked context; at max wraps to 0
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: After overflow, replay any historical signed transaction
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Theoretical replay of all historical transactions

economic_questions: Is 2^256 transactions achievable within any conceivable timeframe?
Could a future chain have different nonce semantics that reduce this number?
validation_requirements: Verify unchecked overflow behavior at type(uint256).max
Confirm no other overflow protection exists

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify unchecked addition wraps at max

threats.title: ERC4337 validateUserOp Deposits ETH Before Signature Validation Allowing Deposit Drain
threats.hypothesis_description: In ERC4337v07.validateUserOp, the wallet transfers missingAccountFunds to the EntryPoint BEFORE validating the signature. While the EntryPoint manages deposits correctly, if the EntryPoint is compromised or has a vulnerability, the wallet's ETH could be drained via repeated validateUserOp calls with invalid signatures and non-zero missingAccountFunds. Even without EntryPoint compromise, the ordering means the wallet sends ETH for operations that may fail validation, though the EntryPoint's own accounting would handle refunds.
threats.investigation_rationale: The deposit-before-validate ordering is standard for ERC4337 but creates a dependency on the EntryPoint's correctness. If the EntryPoint or its accounting has issues, the wallet's balance could be affected.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (missingAccountFunds != 0) { IEntryPoint(entrypoint).depositTo{value: missingAccountFunds}(address(this)); } — ETH sent before this.isValidSignature check

preconditions: ERC4337 is enabled (entrypoint != address(0))
EntryPoint is trusted but may have accounting inconsistencies
Wallet has ETH balance

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: ETH deposited to EntryPoint before signature is validated
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: EntryPoint is immutable and trusted infrastructure

operational_questions: Does the EntryPoint properly refund deposits for failed validations?

Is this ordering required by the ERC4337 specification?

Verify EntryPoint accounting handles the deposit correctly
Check if wallet balance is affected by failed validations
